questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The discovery of penicillin by Alexander Fleming in 1928 is often portrayed as a serendipitous accident: a mold spore drifting through an open window and landing on a forgotten petri dish. However, this narrative, while appealing, obscures the critical role of Fleming's scientific training and methodical observation. He did not merely stumble upon the mold; he recognized its significance because he had spent years studying bacterial cultures and was actively searching for antibacterial agents. The 'accident' was, in truth, a discovery prepared by expertise.

What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Penicillin was discovered entirely by chance.
Fleming's discovery of penicillin, though often called accidental, depended on his scientific expertise and careful observation.
Alexander Fleming was the greatest scientist of the twentieth century.
Mold spores are commonly found in laboratories.
questions.explanation: The passage challenges the 'serendipitous accident' narrative by emphasizing Fleming's training, methodical observation, and years of study—arguing the discovery was 'prepared by expertise.' Option A is what the passage argues against, and C and D are not the main focus.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The discovery of penicillin by Alexander Fleming in 1928 is often portrayed as a serendipitous accident: a mold spore drifting through an open window and landing on a forgotten petri dish. However, this narrative, while appealing, obscures the critical role of Fleming's scientific training and methodical observation. He did not merely stumble upon the mold; he recognized its significance because he had spent years studying bacterial cultures and was actively searching for antibacterial agents. The 'accident' was, in truth, a discovery prepared by expertise.

Which detail from the passage best supports the claim that Fleming's discovery was not purely accidental?
options: A mold spore drifted through an open window.
Fleming had spent years studying bacterial cultures and was actively searching for antibacterial agents.
Penicillin was discovered in 1928.
The petri dish had been forgotten.
questions.explanation: The claim that the discovery was not purely accidental is supported by evidence of Fleming's deliberate preparation—his years of study and active search for antibacterial agents. This shows his mind was prepared to recognize the mold's significance. Options A and D support the accident narrative.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
In her study of the Green Belt Movement in Kenya, environmental historian Wangari Maathai argues that the project's success lay not merely in the millions of trees planted but in its mobilization of rural women as political actors. The movement, she contends, transformed reforestation from a purely ecological endeavor into a vehicle for democratic participation and women's empowerment.

Which title best captures Maathai's argument?
options: A Guide to Tree Planting in East Africa
Roots of Change: How Reforestation Became a Movement for Women's Empowerment
The Ecological Impact of the Green Belt Movement
Kenya's Political History Since Independence
questions.explanation: Maathai's argument emphasizes the dual nature of the movement—ecological and political—with particular focus on women's empowerment. Option B captures this transformative aspect. Options A and C focus only on the ecological side, and D is too broad.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
Paleontologist Dr. Elena Cordova argues that the extinction of the dinosaurs was not caused solely by the Chicxulub asteroid impact 66 million years ago. She points to fossil records showing that dinosaur diversity had already been declining for several million years before the impact, likely due to prolonged volcanic activity in the Deccan Traps that altered global climate.

Which sentence provides the strongest textual evidence for Cordova's argument?
options: Paleontologist Dr. Elena Cordova argues about dinosaur extinction.
The Chicxulub asteroid impact occurred 66 million years ago.
Fossil records show dinosaur diversity had been declining for several million years before the impact.
The Deccan Traps are located in present-day India.
questions.explanation: Cordova's argument is that the asteroid was not the sole cause. The fossil evidence showing pre-impact decline directly supports this by demonstrating that dinosaurs were already in trouble before the asteroid struck. Option A states the argument, and B and D provide context.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
A transportation researcher claims that adding protected bike lanes to major streets increases bicycle commuting rates. Which of the following, if true, would provide the best evidence for this claim?
options: Cyclists report feeling safer on protected bike lanes.
In a study of 15 cities, bicycle commuting increased by an average of 45% within two years of installing protected bike lanes, while cities without new lanes showed no significant change.
Bicycle sales have increased nationwide over the past decade.
Many European cities have extensive bike lane networks.
questions.explanation: The claim concerns increased commuting rates caused by bike lanes. Option B provides a controlled comparison showing a 45% increase in cities with new lanes versus no change in cities without—strong causal evidence. Option A measures perception, not behavior, and C and D are not directly relevant.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
$$\begin{array}{|c|c|}\hline \text{Fruit} & \text{Vitamin C (mg per 100g)} \\ \hline \text{Orange} & 53 \\ \hline \text{Kiwi} & 93 \\ \hline \text{Strawberry} & 59 \\ \hline \text{Grapefruit} & 31 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which conclusion is best supported by the data?
options: Kiwi provides the most vitamin C per 100 grams among the fruits listed.
Oranges are the most nutritious fruit overall.
Grapefruit contains no vitamin C.
All fruits have similar vitamin C content.
questions.explanation: The table shows kiwi with 93 mg per 100g, the highest among the four fruits. Option A is directly supported. Option B makes a broader 'nutrition' claim not in the data, C is false, and D is contradicted by the variation shown.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
A report from the Department of Education states that reading scores for fourth-grade students improved by 6% between 2018 and 2022. The report attributes this improvement to expanded access to early literacy programs.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline \text{Year} & \text{Avg. Reading Score} & \text{\% Enrolled in Pre-K Literacy} \\ \hline 2018 & 218 & 52\% \\ \hline 2020 & 224 & 61\% \\ \hline 2022 & 231 & 68\% \\ \hline\end{array}$$

How does the data in the table relate to the report's claim?
options: The data contradicts the claim because scores improved only slightly.
The data supports the claim by showing that both reading scores and pre-K literacy enrollment increased over the same period.
The data is irrelevant because it does not measure classroom instruction quality.
The data suggests that pre-K enrollment caused the score improvement definitively.
questions.explanation: The table shows both reading scores rising (218 to 231) and pre-K literacy enrollment increasing (52% to 68%) over the same period. This correlation supports the report's attribution, though it does not prove causation. Option D overstates by claiming definitive proof.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
A mayor argues that a curfew for teenagers will reduce juvenile crime rates in the city. Which of the following, if true, would most weaken this argument?
options: Most juvenile offenses in the city occur between 3 PM and 6 PM, immediately after school hours.
Several neighboring cities have implemented curfews.
Parents generally support the proposed curfew.
The city's overall crime rate has been declining for five years.
questions.explanation: The argument assumes that juvenile crime occurs during the hours a curfew would restrict. Option A weakens this by showing most offenses happen in the afternoon, well before any nighttime curfew would take effect, meaning the curfew would not address the majority of juvenile crime.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
Ayesha had always been the one who remembered—birthdays, anniversaries, the names of her colleagues' children. So when she walked past her best friend's desk without so much as a nod on Tuesday morning, the entire office noticed. She spent the day with her door closed, and by evening, her inbox was filled with quiet, tentative messages: 'Is everything okay?'

What can reasonably be inferred about Ayesha's behavior?
options: She has decided to quit her job.
Her uncharacteristic behavior suggests something is troubling her.
She is angry at her best friend.
She was too busy to greet anyone.
questions.explanation: The passage emphasizes that Ayesha's behavior is out of character ('always been the one who remembered'), and the office's collective concern suggests her withdrawal is noticeable and troubling. Option B captures this inference. Options A, C, and D are not supported by the text.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
While many food critics have dismissed the rise of plant-based meat substitutes as a passing trend, food systems scholar Dr. Raj Patel offers a different assessment. He argues that these products, whatever their culinary merits, represent a significant shift in how consumers think about the relationship between diet and climate. Even consumers who do not purchase plant-based meats are increasingly aware of the carbon footprint of animal agriculture, a change in consciousness that Patel sees as irreversible.

Which best describes Patel's perspective on plant-based meat substitutes?
options: He agrees with critics that they are a passing fad.
He values them primarily for their role in shifting consumer consciousness rather than their culinary quality.
He believes they taste better than traditional meat.
He argues they will completely replace animal agriculture within a decade.
questions.explanation: Patel explicitly states that 'whatever their culinary merits,' these products represent a shift in consumer thinking about diet and climate. His focus is on consciousness change, not taste. Options A and D contradict his view, and C is not discussed.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The old man had not spoken to anyone in the nursing home for nearly a week. But when the young volunteer sat down at the piano and began to play a Chopin nocturne—hesitantly, with more feeling than technical precision—he lifted his head. His fingers, resting on the arms of his wheelchair, began to move, tracing patterns in the air as if remembering a language they once knew fluently.

Based on the passage, the old man's reaction most likely suggests that he:
options: Dislikes the sound of piano music.
Has a personal history with music that the piece has awakened.
Wants the volunteer to stop playing.
Is trying to get the volunteer's attention for another reason.
questions.explanation: The detail that his fingers 'trace patterns in the air as if remembering a language they once knew fluently' strongly suggests he was once a pianist or had a deep connection to music. The music has awakened a dormant memory. Options A and C are contradicted by his engagement, and D lacks textual support.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The passage introduces a scientific debate about the purpose of sleep. It first presents the traditional 'restorative theory,' which holds that sleep repairs cellular damage. It then discusses the 'synaptic homeostasis hypothesis,' which proposes that sleep prunes unnecessary neural connections. The final section weighs the evidence for each theory and suggests they may be complementary rather than contradictory.

Which best describes the overall text structure?
options: Presentation of multiple theories followed by a synthesis suggesting they may work together
A chronological history of sleep research
An argument that one theory is clearly superior to the other
A personal narrative about sleep deprivation
questions.explanation: The passage presents two competing theories, evaluates evidence for each, and concludes by suggesting they may be complementary. This is a classic compare-then-synthesize structure. Options B and D are incorrect, and C misrepresents the conclusion.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
Despite hours of testimony, the committee's final report was surprisingly ______; it summarized the complex investigation in fewer than a dozen pages and offered only vague recommendations.
options: comprehensive
succinct
voluminous
meandering
questions.explanation: The context indicates the report was brief despite a lengthy investigation. 'Succinct' means concisely expressed, fitting the description of summarizing complexity in few pages. 'Comprehensive' and 'voluminous' contradict the brevity, and 'meandering' implies unfocused wandering, not concise summary.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The real estate agent described the cottage as 'cozy,' but the buyers recognized the word as a ______ for what was, in reality, a cramped space with barely enough room for a bed and a small table.
options: euphemism
exaggeration
metaphor
alliteration
questions.explanation: The agent uses a pleasant word ('cozy') to describe something negative ('cramped'). This substitution of a mild or positive term for a harsher reality is the definition of a 'euphemism.' 'Exaggeration' is overstatement, 'metaphor' is a comparison, and 'alliteration' is repetition of sounds.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
In linguistics, ______ describes the phenomenon in which two languages in contact influence each other, resulting in the exchange of vocabulary, grammatical structures, or pronunciation patterns. Spanglish, which blends elements of Spanish and English, is a well-known example.
options: language convergence
phonetic drift
syntax shift
semantic bleaching
questions.explanation: The definition—mutual influence between languages in contact, involving vocabulary, grammar, and pronunciation exchange—describes 'language convergence.' 'Phonetic drift' is a gradual sound change, 'syntax shift' only addresses grammar, and 'semantic bleaching' is the weakening of word meaning.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The hospital corridor at 3 AM was a study in fluorescent desolation. The lights buzzed overhead with the insistence of trapped insects while a television mounted in the corner murmured advertisements to an empty waiting room. Somewhere, a door swung shut with a soft pneumatic sigh.

Which best describes the tone created by the author's word choice?
options: Warm and comforting
Sterile and quietly unsettling
Chaotic and loud
Cheerful and optimistic
questions.explanation: The author uses words like 'fluorescent desolation,' 'buzzed,' 'trapped insects,' 'murmured advertisements to an empty room,' and 'sigh' to create a tone that is clinically sterile yet quietly unsettling. Options A and D contradict the imagery, and C overstates (the sounds are described as murmurs and sighs, not chaos).

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
A researcher has compiled the following notes:

— Petra, an archaeological site in Jordan, was the capital of the Nabataean kingdom.
— Its most famous structure, Al-Khazneh, is carved directly into sandstone cliffs.
— The Nabataeans engineered an advanced water management system that allowed the city to thrive in the desert.
— Petra was designated a UNESCO World Heritage site in 1985.

Which choice most effectively combines the information in the notes?
options: Petra is in Jordan and has a famous structure called Al-Khazneh.
Petra, the Nabataean capital in Jordan, features rock-cut architecture such as Al-Khazneh and an advanced water system that enabled desert survival, earning it UNESCO recognition in 1985.
Al-Khazneh is carved into sandstone cliffs.
UNESCO designated Petra as a World Heritage site.
questions.explanation: Option B synthesizes all four notes: the location and historical role (Nabataean capital), the famous architecture (Al-Khazneh), the engineering achievement (water system), and the modern recognition (UNESCO). Options A, C, and D each capture only one or two notes.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
A public health department wants to persuade residents of a rural county to get vaccinated against influenza. Previous vaccination rates in the county have been low. Which approach would most effectively achieve this rhetorical goal?
options: A medical journal article about influenza vaccine efficacy rates.
Mobile vaccination clinics at familiar community locations such as farmers' markets and churches, staffed by local healthcare workers whom residents know and trust.
A billboard campaign with the slogan 'Get Your Flu Shot Today.'
A press release announcing the availability of vaccines at the county health department.
questions.explanation: The goal is to persuade a rural population with historically low vaccination rates. Making vaccines convenient (mobile clinics at familiar locations) and leveraging trusted local relationships (local healthcare workers) directly addresses barriers of access and trust. Options A, C, and D are impersonal and less likely to overcome resistance.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The company's original mission statement emphasized innovation and risk-taking, ______ the revised version, published after the merger, prioritizes stability and incremental growth.
options: whereas
therefore
in addition,
for instance,
questions.explanation: The sentence contrasts the original mission (innovation, risk-taking) with the revised version (stability, incremental growth). 'Whereas' signals this opposition. 'Therefore' implies causation, 'in addition' suggests similarity, and 'for instance' introduces an example.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
______ the expedition faced numerous logistical challenges—equipment failures, supply shortages, and unseasonable weather—the team successfully reached the summit on the twenty-third day.
options: Although
Because
Unless
If
questions.explanation: The sentence presents a contrast: despite challenges, the team succeeded. 'Although' correctly introduces this concessive relationship. 'Because' would make the challenges the reason for success (illogical). 'Unless' and 'If' introduce conditions.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The urban heat island effect causes cities to be significantly warmer than surrounding rural areas, this phenomenon has serious implications for energy consumption and public health during heat waves.
options: NO CHANGE
areas, and this
areas; this
areas this
questions.explanation: The original contains a comma splice—two independent clauses joined only by a comma. Option C correctly uses a semicolon to separate the related clauses. Option B is grammatically acceptable but the semicolon is more effective. Option D creates a fused sentence.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The conference schedule includes workshops on three topics ______ grant writing, public speaking, and data visualization.
options: topics: grant
topics; grant
topics, grant
topics—grant
questions.explanation: A colon is the appropriate punctuation to introduce a list after an independent clause. 'The conference schedule includes workshops on three topics' is complete, and the list specifies the topics. A semicolon separates independent clauses, a comma is insufficient, and an em dash is less conventional.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
______ the archaeologists received permission from the government, they could not begin excavating the site.
options: Until
Although
Because
Whereas
questions.explanation: The sentence describes a condition that must be met before action can begin: permission must precede excavation. 'Until' correctly establishes this temporal condition. 'Although' would suggest contrast, 'Because' would reverse the causality, and 'Whereas' introduces comparison.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The phenomenon known as colony collapse disorder, which has devastated honeybee populations across North America and Europe, ______ the subject of intensive scientific study for nearly two decades.
options: have been
has been
were
are
questions.explanation: The subject is 'phenomenon,' which is singular. The interrupting clause 'which has devastated...' modifies the subject but does not change its number. The singular present perfect 'has been' correctly agrees with 'phenomenon.' Options A, C, and D are plural.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The author submitted the manuscript to several publishers, but ______ was rejected by all of them within a matter of weeks.
options: they
it
he
we
questions.explanation: The antecedent is 'the manuscript,' which is singular and neuter. The singular pronoun 'it' correctly refers to the manuscript. 'They' is plural, 'he' is gendered and suggests a person, and 'we' incorrectly includes the reader.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The five ______ research on climate resilience has been cited in policy documents around the world.
options: scientists
scientists'
scientist's
scientist
questions.explanation: The possessive form is needed to show that the research belongs to the scientists. Since there are five scientists (plural), the apostrophe goes after the 's': 'scientists'.' Option A lacks possession, C is singular possessive, and D is singular without possession.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The decipherment of the Rosetta Stone in 1822 is often credited solely to Jean-François Champollion, but this attribution, while honoring a genuine breakthrough, simplifies a more collaborative history. The stone was discovered by French soldiers in 1799, and copies of its inscriptions circulated among European scholars for two decades before Champollion's work. Thomas Young, an English polymath, had already identified the cartouches as royal names and correctly deduced several hieroglyphic signs. Champollion's genius lay in synthesizing these earlier insights with his own deep knowledge of Coptic to unlock the full system—a cumulative triumph, not an isolated one. Moreover, the trilingual nature of the Rosetta Stone itself was a product of imperial administration under the Ptolemaic dynasty, reminding us that the 'key' to decipherment was forged in a context of conquest and cultural collision.

What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Champollion single-handedly deciphered the Rosetta Stone through his knowledge of Coptic.
The decipherment of the Rosetta Stone was a cumulative process involving multiple scholars, earlier contributions, and a broader imperial context.
Thomas Young deserves more credit than Champollion for the decipherment.
The Rosetta Stone should be returned to Egypt from the British Museum.
questions.explanation: The passage explicitly challenges the 'solely' attribution to Champollion, detailing Young's contributions, the two decades of prior scholarly circulation, and the imperial context. It frames the decipherment as 'a cumulative triumph, not an isolated one.' Option B captures this central argument.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The decipherment of the Rosetta Stone in 1822 is often credited solely to Jean-François Champollion, but this attribution, while honoring a genuine breakthrough, simplifies a more collaborative history. The stone was discovered by French soldiers in 1799, and copies of its inscriptions circulated among European scholars for two decades before Champollion's work. Thomas Young, an English polymath, had already identified the cartouches as royal names and correctly deduced several hieroglyphic signs. Champollion's genius lay in synthesizing these earlier insights with his own deep knowledge of Coptic to unlock the full system—a cumulative triumph, not an isolated one. Moreover, the trilingual nature of the Rosetta Stone itself was a product of imperial administration under the Ptolemaic dynasty, reminding us that the 'key' to decipherment was forged in a context of conquest and cultural collision.

Which detail from the passage best supports the claim that the decipherment was a cumulative achievement?
options: The Rosetta Stone was discovered in 1799.
Champollion had deep knowledge of Coptic.
Thomas Young had already identified royal cartouches and deduced several hieroglyphic signs before Champollion.
The stone contains three scripts.
questions.explanation: The claim of cumulative achievement requires evidence that others contributed before Champollion. Young's prior identification of cartouches and hieroglyphic signs directly demonstrates that Champollion built on earlier work. Options A and D describe the stone, and B describes Champollion alone.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
In her study of the British abolitionist movement, historian Adwoa Mensah challenges the narrative that centers parliamentary debate as the engine of change. She argues that the slave revolts in the Caribbean—particularly the Baptist War in Jamaica (1831–1832)—created a crisis of imperial governance that made abolition politically urgent in ways that decades of parliamentary petitions had not. Mensah's work shifts the geography of abolitionist agency from the metropole to the colony, from the debating chamber to the plantation.

Which title best captures Mensah's argument?
options: Parliamentary Politics and the End of Slavery
Revolts and Reform: How Caribbean Slave Uprisings Drove British Abolition
A Biography of Baptist Missionaries in Jamaica
The Economic Decline of the British Empire
questions.explanation: Mensah argues that Caribbean slave revolts, not parliamentary debate, were the primary driver of abolition. Option B captures this geographic and causal shift. Option A reflects the narrative she challenges, and C and D are not her focus.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
Political scientist Diego Restrepo argues that compulsory voting laws, which exist in countries such as Australia and Belgium, do not merely increase voter turnout but fundamentally alter how political parties campaign. When turnout is nearly universal, he contends, parties cannot rely on mobilizing their base alone; they must appeal to the full spectrum of voters, which moderates political rhetoric and reduces polarization.

Which sentence provides the most direct textual evidence for Restrepo's argument?
options: Compulsory voting laws exist in Australia and Belgium.
Parties must appeal to the full spectrum of voters, which moderates political rhetoric and reduces polarization.
Political scientist Diego Restrepo studies voting behavior.
Voter turnout varies widely across democracies.
questions.explanation: Restrepo's argument is that compulsory voting changes campaign behavior by forcing parties to appeal broadly, moderating rhetoric and reducing polarization. Option B directly states this causal mechanism. Options A, C, and D provide context or background.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
A neuroscientist claims that learning a second language in childhood increases the density of gray matter in the prefrontal cortex. Which of the following, if true, would provide the best evidence?
options: Bilingual children often outperform monolingual children on certain cognitive tests.

MRI scans of 200 participants showed that those who learned a second language before age 10 had, on average, 8% greater gray matter density in the prefrontal cortex than those who learned after age 18 or not at all, controlling for other variables.
Many schools offer dual-language immersion programs.
Adults who learn a second language report feeling mentally sharper.
questions.explanation: The claim is specific: childhood second-language learning increases gray matter density in the prefrontal cortex. Option B provides the best evidence—a controlled neuroimaging study directly measuring the claimed biological effect with appropriate comparison groups. Options A and D are behavioral or subjective, not anatomical. C is about program availability.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline \text{Region} & \text{Population (millions)} & \text{Renewable Energy (\% of total)} \\ \hline \text{Northern Europe} & 33 & 48\% \\ \hline \text{Southern Europe} & 120 & 22\% \\ \hline \text{Western Europe} & 195 & 18\% \\ \hline \text{Eastern Europe} & 290 & 9\% \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which statement is best supported by the data?
options: Eastern Europe has the largest population and the lowest percentage of renewable energy among the regions shown.
Northern Europe uses no fossil fuels.
The total renewable energy used in Western Europe exceeds that of Northern Europe.
All European regions use less than 50% renewable energy.
questions.explanation: Eastern Europe has 290 million people (the largest) and 9% renewable energy (the lowest percentage). Option A is directly supported. Option B is false (48% is not 100%). Option C cannot be determined from percentages alone without total energy consumption data. Option D is supported for the regions shown, but A is the best answer among the choices.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
Sociologist Yuki Tanaka argues that the decline of neighborhood social ties in Tokyo is not primarily a result of digital technology, as popular commentary suggests, but of changes in the built environment. She notes that the replacement of low-rise mixed-use neighborhoods with high-rise residential towers has reduced opportunities for spontaneous social encounters.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline \text{Neighborhood Type} & \text{\% High-Rise} & \text{Avg. Daily Social Interactions} \\ \hline \text{Low-rise mixed-use} & 5\% & 14.2 \\ \hline \text{Moderate mixed-use} & 22\% & 9.8 \\ \hline \text{High-rise dominant} & 68\% & 4.3 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

How does the data in the table relate to Tanaka's argument?
options: The data contradicts the argument by showing social interactions are higher in some areas.
The data supports the argument by showing a negative correlation between the prevalence of high-rise buildings and the frequency of daily social interactions.
The data is irrelevant because it does not measure digital technology use.
The data proves that high-rise buildings cause social isolation.
questions.explanation: Tanaka argues that high-rise development reduces social encounters. The table supports this: as the percentage of high-rise buildings increases (5% → 22% → 68%), average daily social interactions decrease (14.2 → 9.8 → 4.3). Option D overstates by claiming proof of causation.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
A marine biologist argues that reducing plastic pollution in the ocean will allow fish populations to recover. Which of the following, if true, would most strengthen this argument?
options: Plastic debris is found in all of the world's oceans.

A longitudinal study of 30 marine ecosystems found that fish populations increased by an average of 23% within five years of implementing rigorous plastic reduction policies, while overfishing rates remained constant.
Public awareness of plastic pollution has increased significantly in recent years.
Some fish species ingest microplastics, which can cause internal damage.
questions.explanation: The argument claims that reducing plastic pollution causes fish population recovery. Option B provides the strongest evidence: a study showing fish population increases after plastic reduction, with the key confounding variable (overfishing) held constant. Options A and D show the problem but not the recovery effect, and C shows awareness but not ecological outcomes.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The auditors spent six weeks examining every transaction, every receipt, every ledger entry. When they presented their findings to the board, the lead auditor did not sugarcoat the conclusion. She stated flatly that the discrepancies were not errors. Then she distributed a spreadsheet, its cells filled with numbers in red.

What can reasonably be inferred about the audit findings?
options: The auditors found minor bookkeeping mistakes.
The auditors discovered evidence of deliberate financial misconduct.
The company's finances were in perfect order.
The auditors were unable to complete the investigation.
questions.explanation: The auditor's statement that the discrepancies 'were not errors'—combined with 'did not sugarcoat' and the ominous 'numbers in red'—suggests intentional wrongdoing rather than innocent mistakes. Option B captures this inference. Option A contradicts 'not errors,' and C and D are unsupported.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
When economist Ha-Joon Chang writes about the history of trade policy, he does not present free trade as a natural or inevitable evolution. Instead, he meticulously documents how today's wealthy nations—including the United States and the United Kingdom—aggressively protected their infant industries behind high tariff walls before preaching free trade to the developing world. His account is not merely revisionist history; it is an indictment of what he calls the 'bad Samaritan' logic of contemporary economic advice that demands of poorer nations what richer ones never practiced themselves.

Which best describes Chang's perspective on free trade advocacy?
options: He enthusiastically endorses free trade as the best path to development.
He views it as hypocritical, given that wealthy nations themselves developed using protectionist policies.
He is neutral and presents both sides equally.
He argues that developing nations should avoid all trade with wealthy countries.
questions.explanation: Chang documents that wealthy nations used protectionism while developing, then 'preached free trade' to others—a pattern he calls 'bad Samaritan' logic. His tone is critical and exposes hypocrisy. Option A contradicts his argument, C misreads his evident stance, and D overstates.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
For decades, the residents of Sarnia, Ontario, had complained that the chemical plants clustered along the St. Clair River were responsible for elevated rates of certain cancers in the community. Multiple government studies, however, found 'no conclusive link' between industrial emissions and local health outcomes. In 2014, researchers from the University of Toronto deployed a new generation of air-quality monitors—far more sensitive than earlier equipment—and detected levels of benzene, a known carcinogen, at concentrations exceeding federal guidelines by a factor of five in residential areas downwind of the plants. The data was presented to provincial regulators, who ordered the installation of emissions-control technology at the plants in 2016. By 2021, benzene levels had dropped by 78%, and preliminary health data suggested a decline in respiratory hospitalizations.

Which best describes the text structure?
options: A comparison of Sarnia to other industrial communities
A problem-solution structure: community concerns dismissed by authorities, then validated by improved technology, leading to regulatory action and improved outcomes
An argument that all chemical plants should be closed
A chronological history of the St. Clair River
questions.explanation: The passage follows a clear arc: health complaints (problem), government dismissal (conflict), improved monitoring technology (breakthrough), regulatory action (response), and reduced emissions and improved health (resolution). Option B captures this full problem-solution trajectory.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The author of *The Code Breaker* opens each chapter on the development of CRISPR gene-editing technology not with a scientific explanation but with a moment of human drama—an argument between collaborators, a sleepless night before a breakthrough, a patient's story of hope. This structural choice reflects the author's conviction that scientific discovery is not an abstract march of logic but a deeply human endeavor, driven as much by ambition, rivalry, and compassion as by data.

What is the author's primary purpose in structuring the chapters this way?
options: To argue that the science of CRISPR is less important than the personal stories behind it
To emphasize that scientific progress is shaped by human emotions and relationships, not just rational inquiry
To create suspense about whether CRISPR will be approved for medical use
To provide a comprehensive technical explanation of gene-editing mechanisms
questions.explanation: The passage explicitly states that the structure reflects the conviction that scientific discovery is a 'deeply human endeavor, driven by ambition, rivalry, and compassion.' The human-drama openings serve to foreground this theme. Option A overstates, and C and D do not match the purpose described.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The ambassador's speech was ______, simultaneously expressing regret for the incident while carefully avoiding any language that could be interpreted as an admission of legal liability.
options: unequivocal
calibrated
spontaneous
inflammatory
questions.explanation: The context describes a speech that balances two objectives: expressing regret while avoiding legal liability. 'Calibrated' means carefully measured or calculated, fitting this description of precision. 'Unequivocal' means unambiguous (opposite), 'spontaneous' means unplanned (opposite), and 'inflammatory' means provocative (not supported).

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The startup's marketing material described its product as 'disruptive,' a term that in tech circles connotes innovation and forward thinking, but that to traditional manufacturers suggested something altogether less appealing: ______.
options: efficiency
stability
destabilization
tradition
questions.explanation: The passage contrasts the positive connotation of 'disruptive' in tech (innovation) with a negative connotation for traditional manufacturers. 'Destabilization' captures the negative interpretation—something that upsets or breaks existing systems. 'Efficiency' and 'stability' are positive, and 'tradition' would not be the negative interpretation of 'disruptive.'

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
In architecture, a ______ is a central courtyard or atrium, often open to the sky, that serves as the organizing element around which a building or complex is arranged. This design feature, which dates back to ancient Roman houses, continues to appear in contemporary sustainable architecture because it facilitates natural light and ventilation.
options: peristyle
facade
buttress
cornice
questions.explanation: The definition—a central courtyard or atrium open to the sky, serving as an organizing element—matches 'peristyle,' which is a colonnade surrounding a courtyard. 'Facade' is the front exterior face, 'buttress' is a structural support, and 'cornice' is a decorative horizontal projection.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The neighborhood had been described in the guidebook as 'gritty,' but the writer meant this not as a warning but as an invitation. The adjective did not signal danger; it signaled texture—the friction of many languages spoken in the same bakery queue, the rough edges of murals painted on brick, the friction of a place that had not been sanded smooth by gentrification.

Which best describes the function of the figurative language in the passage?
options: To warn tourists to avoid the neighborhood
To redefine 'gritty' from a negative term to one suggesting vibrant, textured authenticity
To argue that all neighborhoods should resist gentrification
To provide statistical data about neighborhood demographics
questions.explanation: The passage explicitly redefines 'gritty'—from a potential warning to an invitation that signals 'texture,' 'friction,' and authenticity. The imagery of languages, murals, and unsanded edges all serve to create this positive redefinition. Options A and D contradict the passage, and C overstates.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
A researcher has compiled the following notes:

— The Voynich manuscript is a 240-page illustrated codex written in an unknown script.
— Carbon dating places its creation between 1404 and 1438.
— Despite decades of analysis by cryptographers, linguists, and computer scientists, no one has definitively deciphered the text.
— Some scholars hypothesize it is a constructed language; others believe it is a sophisticated hoax.
— Its illustrations include botanical diagrams, astronomical charts, and human figures bathing in pools connected by elaborate plumbing.

Which choice most effectively combines the information in the notes?
options: The Voynich manuscript is an undeciphered codex from the 15th century, featuring botanical, astronomical, and anatomical illustrations, with scholars divided on whether it represents a constructed language or an elaborate hoax.
The Voynich manuscript has 240 pages and was created in the 1400s.
No one has deciphered the Voynich manuscript.
The manuscript contains illustrations of plants and stars.
questions.explanation: Option B synthesizes all key notes: the manuscript's nature (undeciphered codex), date (15th century), content (botanical, astronomical, anatomical illustrations), and the scholarly debate (constructed language vs. hoax). Options B, C, and D each capture only fragments of the information.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
A historian writing a book about the 1918 influenza pandemic wants the introduction to convey both the global scale of the crisis and the intimate human experience of loss. Which strategy would best achieve this dual rhetorical goal?
options: Opening with a global mortality estimate followed by a list of countries affected.

Opening with the statistic that the pandemic killed an estimated 50 million people worldwide, then pivoting immediately to a detailed account of a single family in Philadelphia who lost four members in ten days.
A chronological summary of public health measures taken in 1918.
A comparison of the 1918 pandemic to the COVID-19 pandemic.
questions.explanation: The goal is to convey both global scale and intimate human experience. Option B achieves this by starting with the massive statistic (50 million) to establish scale, then pivoting to a specific family's story for intimacy. Option A covers scale only, C is administrative not intimate, and D introduces a different pandemic.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The initial results of the drug trial were inconclusive, with patients showing highly variable responses; ______, the researchers decided to expand the sample size and extend the observation period before drawing any conclusions.
options: consequently,
in contrast,
moreover,
for example,
questions.explanation: The inconclusive results and variable responses are the reason the researchers decided to expand the trial. 'Consequently' indicates this cause-and-effect relationship. 'In contrast' suggests opposition, 'moreover' adds information, and 'for example' introduces an illustration.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
______ the cave's narrow passages had discouraged explorers for decades, the discovery of a previously hidden entrance in 2019 revealed chambers containing prehistoric art that rewrote the region's archaeological timeline.
options: Although
Because
Unless
Whereas
questions.explanation: The sentence presents a contrast: narrow passages discouraged exploration, yet a hidden entrance led to major discoveries. 'Although' correctly introduces this concessive relationship. 'Because' would make the discouragement the reason for discovery (illogical).

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The climate models developed in the early 2000s failed to predict the rapid acceleration of Arctic ice loss; their projections now appear conservative in light of satellite data from the past decade.
options: NO CHANGE
loss, their
loss their
loss, and their
questions.explanation: The sentence correctly uses a semicolon to join two related independent clauses. The second clause elaborates on the significance of the first. Option B creates a comma splice, C creates a fused sentence, and D adds unnecessary coordination.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
To understand the novel's central theme, the reader must pay close attention not only to what the narrator says but also to what she ______.
options: omitted
omits
is omitting
had omitted
questions.explanation: The sentence requires parallel structure with 'says,' which is present tense. 'Omits' maintains this present tense parallelism. 'Omitted' is past tense, 'is omitting' is continuous, and 'had omitted' is past perfect—all breaking the parallelism.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The discovery of gravitational waves in 2015 confirmed a prediction Einstein had made a century earlier, ______ it also opened an entirely new field of observational astronomy.
options: and
but
because
unless
questions.explanation: The sentence presents two related but distinct ideas: confirmation of an old prediction and the opening of something new. 'But' signals this subtle contrast between looking backward (confirming Einstein) and forward (opening new possibilities). 'And' would merely add without the nuance of contrast.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The prairie ecosystem, which once stretched across millions of acres of North America and supported countless species of grasses, wildflowers, and grazing animals, ______ now reduced to less than 1% of its original range.
options: are
have been
is
were
questions.explanation: The subject is 'ecosystem,' which is singular. The long interrupting clause 'which once stretched...' modifies the subject but does not change its number. The singular present tense 'is' correctly agrees. Options A, B, and D are plural.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
Neither the novel's intricate plot nor its memorable characters ______ enough to compensate for what most reviewers agreed was a deeply unsatisfying ending.
options: was
were
is
has been
questions.explanation: When 'neither...nor' connects two subjects, the verb agrees with the nearest subject. Here, 'characters' is plural and closer to the verb, so the plural verb 'were' is correct. Options A, C, and D are singular.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
After the university merged its two biology departments, the newly consolidated unit announced that ______ would be moving into the recently completed research facility on the west campus.
options: they
it
we
he
questions.explanation: The antecedent is 'the newly consolidated unit,' which is a singular collective entity. The singular pronoun 'it' correctly refers to the unit. 'They' is plural, 'we' incorrectly includes the reader, and 'he' is gendered and singular but inappropriate for an institutional unit.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The documentary includes interviews with several veterans, but the director explained that the choice to focus on the ______ personal stories rather than military strategy was intentional.
options: soldiers
soldiers'
soldier's
soldier
questions.explanation: The possessive form is needed to show the stories belong to the veterans. Since the context indicates multiple veterans (several veterans were interviewed), the plural possessive 'soldiers'' is correct. Options A lacks possession, C is singular possessive, and D is singular without possession.
